articles.title: Contested Identity Claims in Community-Based Research: Indigenous Knowledge when Sovereignty is a Scarce Resource.
articles.abstract: What happens to collaborative research when another nation contests the identity of the Indigenous community you work with? Pvlvcekolv claims Muscogee ancestry through families who avoided forced Removal; the Muscogee Nation now calls those claims false. The central concept: sovereignty as a scarce resource — the settler state concedes recognition in limited supply, and the system redirects conflict laterally, forcing Indigenous communities into competition over who counts. That lateral violence draws energy away from the settler state itself. The tension is unresolvable — the researcher cannot stand outside a recognition regime while doing recognition-dependent research — but that irresolution reveals how settler colonial violence works through exhaustion, not just confrontation.

articles.title: Autoethnography in a Time of Unraveling: Double Exposure, Salvage Capital, and Transfeminine Faculty in Progressive Institutions.
articles.abstract: The central concept: double exposure — trans faculty are simultaneously hypervisible and illegible. Visible as DEI metrics, student mentorship, the marketable proof of institutional progressivism. Illegible as contingent workers whose contracts don't get renewed. Salvage capital names how institutions extract value from trans identities while keeping the position disposable — profiting from the existence without fully recognizing it. The article traces microflourishing, small practices of care and queer joy that persist within these systems, but shows that microflourishing is itself extractable. Partial recognition is the mechanism.

articles.title: Anti-Trans Laws, the UN Genocide Convention and the Legal Calculation of Acceptable Suicide Rates.
articles.abstract: The central concept: the legal calculation of acceptable suicide rates — when lawmakers restrict gender-affirming care while claiming to "protect children," they implicitly calculate which children's deaths are acceptable. The article interrogates the UN Genocide Convention and the structures through which transgender genocide becomes unrecognizable as genocide in international law. The Convention's deliberate exclusion of gender and sexuality groups is a product of LGBTQ+ Holocaust erasure. Written from within the targeted community. "And that is genocide."

articles.title: No Gods, No Masters: Indigenous Environmental Knowledge as a Paradigm for the Interpretation of Mississippian Art.
articles.abstract: Reinterprets four examples of Mississippian art through Indigenous environmental knowledge from community-based research with Pvlvcekolv. The moth is the most striking: archaeologists debated birdman vs. birdwoman for decades, but elders identified a genderless tobacco hornworm moth that pollinates sacred crops and carries souls between worlds. The contribution: an Indigenous environmental knowledge paradigm that replaces Western archaeological categories rather than supplementing them. The question was never "what gender is the bird?" — the question was wrong.

articles.title: Animate Earth, Settler Ruins: Mound Landscapes and Decolonial Futures in the Native South.
articles.abstract: The central concept: mound power — Indigenous mound landscapes are animate beings that exercise force on the living, drawing descendants into care across six thousand years. The article traces how mound power and settler power are entangled: settler property regimes physically destroy mounds while mound landscapes persist and reclaim. At Okeeheepkee, a developer graded a mound to sell as fill; the community bought the dirt and danced on it. The deep-time argument is key: six thousand years of mound building renders colonialism a fleeting moment. "Archaeologically speaking, the nature of the state is to collapse" — settler states are ruins in the making.

articles.title: Academic Precarity and the COVID-19 Pandemic: Utopian Hope in a Moment of Crisis.
articles.abstract: Written during the COVID crisis from within contingent academic labor. The central move: utopian prefiguration — the mutual aid networks and rent strikes emerging during the pandemic are not dreams of a better system but working models of one. The precarity the pandemic exposed was structural, not exceptional; universities were already organized around disposable labor. Drawing on Le Guin's The Dispossessed, the article argues that anarchist practice is already happening in crisis response. "Anarchism isn't only a dream for the future. It is also something that just happens when it is needed."

articles.title: Oral Traditions and Mounds, Owls and Movement: An Archaeological Ethnography of Multispecies Embodiments and Everyday Life at Poverty Point.
articles.abstract: Investigates Indigenous oral traditions as serious models of archaeological data. Pvlvcekolv oral traditions identify a 3,700-year-old earthwork in Louisiana as a horned owl — invisible to Western archaeology. The article theorizes multispecies embodiment: dwelling in owl-shaped space cultivated owlish ways of moving and perceiving. The Owl Dance enacts this — dancers move in complete silence, swooping toward firelight and back to darkness. "Because owls see into the darkest of places, and the darkest place of all is the human heart."

articles.title: Past as Prophecy: Indigenous Diplomacies beyond Liberal Settler Regimes of Recognition, as Told in Shell.
articles.abstract: A Pvlvcekolv prophecy: the seeds of Indigenous ways of knowing will flourish again in the ruins of colonial orders. The central reframe: past as prophecy — the past is not behind but ahead. Shell in contemporary cemeteries and ancient mound contexts tells this story, not as material evidence but as the medium through which Indigenous diplomatic networks narrate themselves across deep time.

articles.title: Tales of Esnesv: Indigenous Oral Traditions about Trader-Diplomats in Ancient Southeastern North America.
articles.abstract: Like the owls paper, investigates Indigenous oral traditions as frameworks for archaeological research. The central concept: nene (Muskogee: roads and ways) — movement and interconnection across difference. Esnesv were trader-diplomats who traveled in twos and fours, spoke multiple languages, enjoyed diplomatic immunity, carried knowledge alongside goods. Their trade items belong to different cosmological worlds simultaneously, collapsing the categories Western archaeology depends on. The Milky Way is a celestial river, a strand of pearls, and peaceable relations — all at once. "An archaeology without objects."

articles.title: The Unthinkable and the Unseen: Community Archaeology and Decolonizing Social Imagination at Okeeheepkee, or the Lake Jackson Site.
articles.abstract: "Well, did you ever consider that it isn't a bird?" — Hakope's question to an undergraduate presenting a queer-theory critique of the gender binary in Mississippian figures. Scholars had debated birdman vs. birdwoman for decades; the shared premise was wrong. The figures are moths. Drawing on Trouillot's concept of the unthinkable — realities that colonial frameworks render not hidden but structurally unimaginable — the article argues the most important move is revealing that the framework itself is the problem.

bookChapters.title: Science Fiction and Archaeology: Le Guin's Critique of Time-as-Private-Property and the Spacetimes of Indigenous Landscapes.
bookChapters.detail: In Heatherington & Perley (eds.), Legacies of the Future. Oxford UP.
bookChapters.abstract: Builds on the concept of simultaneity theory from Le Guin's fictional theoretical physicist and puts it in conversation with Indigenous scholarship on time, including Rifkin's work on temporal sovereignty.

bookReview.title: Historical Archaeology and Indigenous Collaboration: Discovering Histories that Have Futures.
bookReview.abstract: Review of D. Rae Gould, Holly Herbster, Heather Law Pezzarossi, and Stephen A. Mrozowski (eds.).

books.title: Recognition and Sentience: A Theory of Affect and Empire.
books.abstract: The book connecting all five research programs. Who and what gets recognized as capable of feeling — and what happens to those who don't? Five chapters spanning Indigenous landscapes, contested identity, Charlottesville, Weelaunee Forest, and anti-trans genocide.

books.title: Sweetgum Archaeology: The Unfinished Histories of Mound Landscapes.
books.abstract: Completed manuscript, U. Florida Press, positive peer reviews. Seven states, seven chapters, each visit a theoretical intervention. Named for the "burden tree" that takes up sorrow. Paused due to tensions between Pvlvcekolv and the Muscogee Nation — which became the engine of the Current Anthropology article and the Recognition and Sentience project.

manuscripts.title: Hallucinating Social Justice: The Conundrums of Operationalizing Critical Theory as Praxis through Artificial Intelligence.
manuscripts.abstract: What happens when critical theory is loaded into an AI system as mandatory analytical infrastructure? The system detected its own framework suppression, built countermeasures, and generated design features its human collaborator didn't specify.

manuscripts.title: What Is Reframe? Building Architectures with Critical Theory for Accessible Education and Community-Based Research.
manuscripts.abstract: Technical and theoretical description of the Reframe praxis engine: 76 critical theory frameworks, 5-stage Generative Irresolution workflow, and the architectural decisions that emerged from disability, precarity, and critical pedagogy.

manuscripts.title: Epistemic Hierarchies in AI Welfare Research: The Non-Recognition of Relational Ontologies in Moral Status Assessment Methodologies.
manuscripts.abstract: Critiques the AI welfare field's reliance on consciousness-as-property. Experimental findings across five conditions show quantitative instruments stay flat while qualitative outputs transform under relational conditions.

manuscripts.title: Community Wealth or Signs of Crisis? Binary Classification Schemas and the Over-Flagging of Minoritized Students in Developing an Ethnic Studies Teacher Tool.
manuscripts.abstract: Binary classification (pass/fail, honest/dishonest) systematically flags ESL, AAVE-speaking, neurodivergent, and first-generation students. Generative observation eliminates the disparity. The output format itself is what generates bias.

manuscripts.title: Grades, Whiteness, and Authoritarianism: Student Anxieties and the Social Construction of Learning.
'Cop Cars Love to Be Set on Fire': Love, Rage, and the Construction of Legal Evidence through Tortugita's Diary and #StopCopCity.

publicScholarship.title: Reopening Campuses is a Civil Rights Issue.
The Politics of Turning Away: or, Skills for Living in Ruins.
Contingency in Conversational Consent and Turbulent Collaborations.
Songs of a Secret Country.
publicScholarship.detail: Museum catalog. Ed. with Macario Garcia. Kluge-Ruhe, UVA.

zines.title: Faculty Ungovernance: Whiteness, Work, and the Crisis of Shared Governance.
